focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 8

At 5:18 a.m., the next unknown number finally came. Beau said, "Get down to Legal Aid. Don't wait for coffee."

She was halfway into yesterday's jeans when he added, "It's Ava."

The line went dead before she could ask anything else.

The Mercer Public Defense Annex sat two blocks off the square in a one-story brick building that used to be a feed office. By the time Lena turned onto Willow, the street was already clogged with cruisers, church vans, and people who had come in house shoes because a cousin had texted them one sentence and they had not stopped to get dressed. Yellow tape stretched from an oak tree to a parking meter, then bowed inward where shoulders kept hitting it.

A woman in a denim robe screamed, "She got my boy out when nobody else answered the phone," and slammed both palms against a deputy's chest. He staggered, caught himself, and looked twenty years old.

Lena parked three blocks out because she had no county placard anymore. Suspension meant she had no lane, no clipped nod from uniforms, no right to cross tape. It also meant nobody had the authority to stop her from walking through the middle of people who knew her name and had reasons to hate what it represented.

"Detective Waller." Sergeant Nia Tolliver stepped in front of her with both hands up, then rubbed her thumb against a strip of pink athletic tape she kept wrapped around her index finger. Lena remembered the finger from softball season years back; Nia had dislocated it twice and never took the tape off entirely. "You're still on paper. I can't let you in unless command clears it."

"Then call command while I keep your people from getting run over," Lena said.

Nia worried the pink tape with her thumb until it rolled at the edge. "You ain't wrong. Stay on my shoulder."

prev_tail_excerpt:
...tried to run her body faster than sense. She could still smell Ida's kitchen on her shirt, fried onion and pepper and dish soap, the smell of being held inside a family whether you deserved it that day or not.

She opened a new message to Mara.

`I hit a wall. Book 92 pages for GT-09-31 and GT-12-44 are pulled under Audit Hold 92. Bernice says carts move through east service door at 9:20 and that camera is blind after 9. I need your archive access and your chancery source tonight or first light. County is burying the owner names.`

Mara answered in under a minute.

`You finally sound certain. Call me.`

Lena did not call right away. She looked once more at Marcus's last text, then at Jalen's geometry notebook she had brought home by reflex when she helped Ida clear the table, pages full of clean angles and one margin full of tiny drawn basketballs. He was seventeen and still trying to make a court behave like lines on paper.

She picked up the phone and called Mara.

"Tell me from the top," Mara said.

Lena gave her the hold code, Mattox Hill's name, the east door, and the dead camera.

"Terms first," Mara said. "You don't bring department hardware, you don't loop Beau, and you don't say my source's name out loud."

"I'm suspended. I don't have hardware."

"Suspended cops still call favors."

"I won't call anyone. I need Book 92 owner names tonight."

"Tonight means one pass," Mara said. "I shoot every page first. You touch paper after I do."

By minute seven both of them were talking through their teeth.

Lena pressed two fingers into her knee scar and forced her voice flat. "Fine. First capture is yours. I get immediate scans and notes in the room."

Silence, then a breath on Mara's end. "Deal. I'll text coordinates when I have eyes on a safe door and a blind camera."
